student_name: João Guilherme da Costa e Silva
hub_subtitle: Business English técnico para reuniões com fornecedores internacionais, normas e entrevistas

lesson.menu_title: Performing Under Pressure -- The International Job Interview
lesson.menu_desc: The interview you did not want to have again: cleft sentences, the language of the résumé and twenty minutes with a Belgian hiring manager -- 36 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 5 -- Performing Under Pressure
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- João Guilherme | Aula 5 -- Performing Under Pressure

listenings.text: João, this is Sofie Claes. I said I would call you after the panel, and I would rather you heard this from me than from a recruiter. First, the decision. We are taking you to the final round, and the two people who were most difficult to convince are the two who are now most in favor. Second, why. What convinced the panel was not the seven years, it was the way you set out the San Francisco commissioning. You told us what the problem was, what you decided, and what it cost. That is a track record, and very few candidates give us one. Third, the part you will want to hear and will not enjoy. Under pressure, you retreat into detail. When my technical director asked you about competing priorities, you gave him three minutes of relay logic. What he wanted was one sentence about how you decide what to drop. In the final round, the questions will be less technical and more uncomfortable, and the temptation will be to burn the midnight oil on the specification. Do not. Prepare the four sentences that make your value proposition, and prepare the one about the gap in your English, because I will ask you about it, and I will ask you in front of the board. You will not throw in the towel. You did not throw in the towel today.

Before your next interview in English, three things, because engineers get these wrong more often than anybody else. First. Nobody is testing your English. They are testing whether the engineering survives the English. A hiring manager can forgive a wrong preposition. What she cannot forgive is not knowing what you actually did on the project, and a candidate who buries the decision inside the description is a candidate who did not make the decision. Set out the problem, the choice, the outcome. Three sentences. Second. Cleft sentences are the cheapest confidence you can buy. Instead of saying I have experience with international suppliers, say what I bring to this role is seven years of managing international suppliers under contractual pressure. The information is identical. The weight is not. It buys you half a second to think, and it tells the panel where to look. Third, and this is the one that costs people the job. When you do not know, say so, and then say what you would do to find out. A subject matter expert is not somebody who knows everything. It is somebody the team trusts when the answer is not in the manual. And one last word. Every panel has one question designed to make you uncomfortable. It is not a trap. It is the whole interview. Prepare that answer, out loud, before you sit down.

patterns.title: Four ways to point the panel at what matters
cards: You decide what the listener focuses on, and you buy half a second to think. "What I bring to this role is seven years in railway signaling."
You put the spotlight on one fact
The stress falls on the time, the place or the person you chose. "It was in San Francisco that I first ran a full commissioning cycle."
The value proposition, in one line
The sentence every panel is waiting for, and almost nobody prepares. "What sets me apart is that I read the standard and I run the site."
You control the story of a decision
It answers a question they have not asked yet, before they ask it badly. "The reason I moved into signaling is that safety is where engineering becomes non-negotiable."

patterns.text: A cleft sentence is not decoration. It is a decision about what the panel hears first. Use two or three in an answer. Use six and you sound rehearsed.

items.situation: "So, tell me about yourself." Ninety seconds. Do not start with the day you were born.
tips: What I do at Motiva is own the signaling design for metro line extensions, from specification to commissioning.
What I bring to this role is seven years of managing German, French, Spanish and Chinese suppliers under contractual pressure.

items.situation: "What makes you the right person for this role?" One sentence, then the evidence.
tips: What sets me apart is that I read the standard and I run the site.
It was in San Francisco, in 2023, that I first did both on the same project.

items.situation: "Tell me about a time you managed a conflict with an international supplier." Problem, decision, outcome.
tips: One example that comes to mind is a supplier who missed three consecutive deadlines on a signaling package.
What I did was put forward a recovery plan before anybody mentioned the penalty clause.

items.situation: "How do you handle competing priorities?" She wants one sentence about how you decide, not three minutes of detail.
tips: The way I approach this kind of challenge is to ask which item is on the critical path for safety approval.
What I drop is never the hold point. What I renegotiate is the date.

items.situation: "You mentioned a period where you were not using English intensively. How will that affect you here?" Do not apologize. Account for it.
tips: What I learned from that experience was that fluency is a habit, not a memory.
It was that interview, in March, that made me rebuild the habit deliberately, and I have been in weekly training since.

items.situation: "Do you have any questions for us?" Never say no. Ask something only an engineer would ask.
tips: What I would like to understand is how the technical lead and the project manager split the decision on a non-conformance here.
And who the subject matter expert is when the standard is silent.

bank.label: Opening the interview
items: What I do at Motiva is own the signaling design for metro line extensions, from specification to commissioning.
What I bring to this role is seven years of managing international suppliers under contractual pressure.
Let me set out my background in three steps: the standards, the suppliers, and the site.

bank.label: Making the value proposition
items: What sets me apart is that I read the standard and I run the site.
It was in San Francisco that I first ran a full commissioning cycle with a client on-site.
What the panel usually wants to know is whether the engineering survives the deadline. In my case, it has.

bank.label: The behavioral answer
items: One example that comes to mind is a supplier who missed three consecutive deadlines.
What I learned from that experience was that a corrective action with a date is worth more than an apology.
The way I approach this kind of challenge is to separate the schedule risk from the safety risk.

bank.label: The uncomfortable question
items: I would say my greatest strength in this area is resilience: I did not throw in the towel, I rebuilt the habit.
What I would do differently today is prepare the four sentences that carry the whole answer.
That is not in my experience yet. What I would do is go to the standard first, and then to the supplier's subject matter expert.
